topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crumpled invitation lay on the kitchen counter, a piece of flimsy paper mocking me. I picked it up, the edges already softened from my repeated handling, and let out a low, involuntary sigh that seemed to vibrate the stale air. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck bunching up as I scanned the printed text. *“Join us for a celebration on Saturday…”* The rest of the details were obscured by a hastily scrawled note in red ink: “Cancelled.” A slow, heavy wave of disappointment washed over me, leaving a dull ache behind my ribs. I dropped the paper back onto the counter with a soft thud, the sound echoing the sudden deflation in my chest. I didn't bother to look at the calendar; the thought of the planned merriment was simply too much effort to process right now. I started rinsing a plate, the water running too loudly, a deliberate attempt to fill the silence with irritating noise.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the message blinking mockingly in the center of my laptop. My shoulders slumped forward, pulling my shirt taut across my back. It was impossible. After all the planning, the coordinated efforts, the sheer amount of anticipation I’d invested—all wiped clean with a single, curt notification. A muscle twitched near my temple. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound sharp and sudden in the quiet apartment. I paced three steps to the window, my boots scuffing the hardwood floor. The city lights outside seemed too bright, too indifferent to my sudden deflation. "Of course," I muttered to the empty room, the words tasting like ash. I turned abruptly, walking toward the sofa and sinking into it with a heavy groan, deliberately choosing the most uncomfortable position available.

[story 3]

The phone call had been the final straw. I’d been waiting for confirmation all afternoon, my patience thinning like old thread. When the voice on the other end delivered the news—that the event was off—I didn't raise my voice. Instead, I just let the silence stretch, letting the weight of the news settle onto my chest like a physical burden. I rubbed the bridge of my nose with two fingers, the gesture tight and repetitive. My eyes focused on a dust mote dancing in a shaft of sunlight cutting across the floor. Everything felt unnecessarily loud, everything too bright. I stood up abruptly, needing movement, and walked into the hallway, deliberately moving with a slow, deliberate stiffness, as if every joint protested the effort.
